title: SOGS Pipeline: YouTube Video → GLOMAP → SOGS → Compressed Grid
description:
## Lab 6B: SOGS Pipeline

This lab runs the Self-Organizing Gaussians (SOGS) pipeline on the same Bamburgh Castle YouTube video used in Lab 6A — giving you a direct side-by-side comparison of standard 3DGS and SOGS compression on identical source material.

### Pipeline Overview

```
YouTube URL (same as Lab 6A)
    ↓  yt-dlp + ffmpeg (reuse from Lab 6A, or re-extract)
images/ (~1,179 frames @ 640×272)
    ↓  COLMAP feature_extractor + exhaustive_matcher
database.db
    ↓  GLOMAP mapper
sparse/0/ (585 cameras, 14,452 3D points)
    ↓  SOGS training
       --optimizer_type sparse_adam
       hydra.run.dir=./sogs_output
sogs_output/  (ordered attribute grids)
    ↓  Compression + entropy analysis
compressed_grids/  (PNG / float16 arrays)
    ↓  PSNR / SSIM / LPIPS evaluation
metrics.json  (compare to Lab 6A baseline)
```

### Timing Budget (Colab T4)

| Stage | Approximate Duration |
|---|---|
| Video download + frame extraction | ~30 seconds (skip if reusing Lab 6A) |
| COLMAP feature extraction + matching | ~3 minutes |
| GLOMAP reconstruction | ~70 minutes |
| SOGS training (SOM + PLAS + refinement) | ~25 minutes |
| Compression + entropy analysis | ~5 minutes |
| Metric evaluation | ~5 minutes |
| **Total** | **~100 minutes** |

### Part A: Run the SOGS Pipeline

Install SOGS dependencies (different from standard 3DGS), run COLMAP/GLOMAP on the Bamburgh Castle video, and launch SOGS training. Confirm the output directory contains ordered attribute grids.

### Part B: Compression & Entropy Analysis

For each attribute group (positions, SH coefficients, scales, rotations):

1. Compare the raw float32 size vs. quantized int8 size.
2. Measure byte entropy before and after SOM ordering — does ordering reduce entropy?
3. Apply gzip to both opacity-sorted and SOM-sorted data and compare compressed sizes.
4. Plot the empirical byte distribution for the DC SH channel under each ordering strategy.

### Part C: Head-to-Head Metric Comparison

Using the 1/8 LLFF holdout:

1. Compute PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS for Lab 6A (standard 3DGS) renders.
2. Compute the same metrics for SOGS renders.
3. Build a comparison DataFrame and plot the PSNR–file-size tradeoff curve.
4. Identify which held-out views show the largest quality difference between methods.

### Part D: Research Extensions

Choose one:

- **Alternative datasets**: Download the Tanks and Temples Truck sequence and compare reconstruction quality vs. the YouTube video.
- **SOM hyperparameter sweep**: Vary the SOM neighborhood radius schedule and measure its effect on PSNR and gzip ratio.
- **Quantization ablation**: Train SOGS with 4-bit, 6-bit, and 8-bit SH quantization and plot the rate-distortion curve.
- **Video codec compression**: Export SOM-sorted attribute grids as a video and measure HEVC compression efficiency vs. PNG.

Relevant datasets for further research: [IMW 2020 benchmark data](https://www.cs.ubc.ca/~kmyi/imw2020/data.html) provides multi-view stereo sequences with ground-truth depth for rigorous evaluation.
objectives: Install SOGS dependencies in a Colab environment and run the end-to-end pipeline from a YouTube URL through GLOMAP reconstruction to SOGS-ordered attribute grids.
Measure byte entropy of Gaussian attribute arrays under opacity-sorted vs. SOM-sorted orderings and quantify the correlation between ordering strategy and gzip compression ratio.
Compare PSNR, SSIM, and LPIPS between standard 3DGS (Lab 6A) and SOGS on the same source video using the 1/8 LLFF holdout protocol.
Construct a file-size vs. PSNR tradeoff curve across multiple SOGS quantization settings and identify the knee point where quality degrades rapidly.
Identify which scene regions (near vs. far, textured vs. smooth) benefit most from SOM ordering by examining per-view metric differences.
Extend the pipeline to at least one additional dataset or hyperparameter configuration and document the results in a comparison table.
